coredata.dc:title: A facile reflux method to produce ternary CQDs/P25/NH2−MIL-125 for photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue under simulated solar light
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Hsiao, Wen-Chieh
Tseng, Chia-Hao
Huang, Chao-Wei
coredata.dc:description:
Background To enhance the photocatalytic performance of commercial titanium dioxide (P25), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and NH2−MIL-125 were utilized to address its limitations arising from the large bandgap and small specific surface area. The CQDs were employed to broaden the range of solar light absorption, while NH2−MIL-125 was used to disperse P25 with a higher specific surface area.
Methods A facile reflux method was conducted to synthesize CQD/P25/NH2−MIL-125 composite materials. The up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) effect of CODs was leveraged to convert longer wavelengths of light into shorter ones, facilitating P25 and NH2−MIL-125 to enhance photocatalytic performance.
Significant findings The UCPL effect of CQDs not only broadened the light absorption range but also retarded the recombination of photo-induced electron-hole pairs, leading to improved photocatalytic reactions. Furthermore, the effect of additional amounts of CQDs was investigated, and it was found that 1wt% CQDs/P25/NH2−MIL-125 followed first-order kinetics, with the highest rate constant (k1) of 0.0141.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalyst
Titanium dioxide
Metal-organic framework
Carbon quantum dots
Dye degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selective adsorption of mixed MB/RhB solutions
Photocatalytic performance and kinetics analysis of CPNM125(RP)−0.5, 1.0, 1.5

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC), Taiwan, for the financial support under grant number MOST 110–2221-E-006–217-MY3. Special thanks to Prof. Li-Heng Kao (Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering, National Kaohsiung University of Science and Technology) and Prof. Chung-Wei Kung (Department of Chemical Engineering, National Cheng Kung University) for supporting the resource of instruments. The authors also thank the facility support from NSTC 112–2740-M-006–001 and MOST111–2731-M-110–001.

The authors gratefully acknowledge the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) , Taiwan, for the financial support under grant number MOST 110–2221-E-006–217-MY3 . Special thanks to Prof. Li-Heng Kao (Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering, National Kaohsiung University of Science and Technology) and Prof. Chung-Wei Kung (Department of Chemical Engineering, National Cheng Kung University) for supporting the resource of instruments. The authors also thank the facility support from NSTC 112–2740-M-006–001 and MOST111–2731-M-110–001 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of (a) P25, NM125(RP), PNM125(RP), and CPNM125(RP)−0.5, −1.0, −1.5; (b) a detailed perspective of CPNM125(RP)−0.5, −1.0, −1.5.
SEM images of (a) P25, (b) NMIL125(RP), (c) PNMIL125(RP), (d) CPNMIL125(RP)−0.5, (e) CPNMIL125(RP)−1.0, and (f) CPNMIL125(RP)−1.5.
TEM images of CPNMIL125(RP)−1.0 sample at a magnification of (a) 40,000x, (b) 120,000x, and (c) 500,000x.
(a) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and (b) pore distribution of NM125(RP), PNM125(RP), and CPNM125(RP)−0.5, −1.0, −1.5.
FTIR spectra of NM125(RP), PNM125(RP), and CPNM125(RP)−1.0.
XPS spectra of NMIL125(RP), PNM125 (RP), and CPNM125(RP)−1.0.
(a) UV–Vis DRS spectra and (b) the Tauc plot calculation for NM125(RP), PNM125(RP), and CPNM125 (RP)−0.5, −1.0, −1.5.
(a) The zeta potentials of NM125(RP) and (b) Nyquist plots from EIS of the samples.
(a) PL emission spectra of NH2−MIL-125 (RP), PNH2−MIL-125 (RP), and CPNH2−MIL-125 (RP)−0.5, −1.0, −1.5; (b) the proposed mechanism based on the PL results over different samples.
The up-conversion PL spectra of Aniline-derived CQDs.
The EPR analysis of (a) CPNM125(RP)−1.0 under light irradiation and in the dark; (b) NM125(RP), PNM125(RP), and CPNM125(RP)−1.0 under light irradiation.

(a) The changes in absorbance of a mixed RhB/MB dye solution using NM125(RP) for selective adsorption; (b) the C/C0 ratio of the mixed RhB/MB dye solution; (c) a schematic representation of NM125(RP)’s selective adsorption capability toward the mixed MB/RhB solution.
(a)The photocatalytic performances for MB degradation and (b) first-order kinetics analysis using CPNM125(RP)−0.5, −1.0, and −1.5.
The proposed mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation of MB using 1.0 wt% CQDs/P25/NH2−MIL-125.
Specific surface area, pore volume, and pore size of the samples.
The resistance of the as-synthesized samples.
First-order kinetic analysis for the photocatalytic degradation of MB using CPNM125(RP)−0.5, −1.0, and −1.5.
Comparison of MOF photocatalysts for the removal of pollutants.

head.ce:title: A facile reflux method to produce ternary CQDs/P25/NH2−MIL-125 for photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue under simulated solar light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering, National Kaohsiung University of Science and Technology, Kaohsiung 80778, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering
National Kaohsiung University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemical and Materials Engineering, National Kaohsiung University of Science and Technology, Kaohsiung 80778, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Engineering Science, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan 70101, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Engineering Science, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan 70101, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To enhance the photocatalytic performance of commercial titanium dioxide (P25), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and NH2−MIL-125 were utilized to address its limitations arising from the large bandgap and small specific surface area. The CQDs were employed to broaden the range of solar light absorption, while NH2−MIL-125 was used to disperse P25 with a higher specific surface area.

A facile reflux method was conducted to synthesize CQD/P25/NH2−MIL-125 composite materials. The up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) effect of CODs was leveraged to convert longer wavelengths of light into shorter ones, facilitating P25 and NH2−MIL-125 to enhance photocatalytic performance.

The UCPL effect of CQDs not only broadened the light absorption range but also retarded the recombination of photo-induced electron-hole pairs, leading to improved photocatalytic reactions. Furthermore, the effect of additional amounts of CQDs was investigated, and it was found that 1wt% CQDs/P25/NH2−MIL-125 followed first-order kinetics, with the highest rate constant (k1) of 0.0141.

ce:section.ce:para: The utilization of fossil fuels, pesticides, and fertilizers in human civilization has led to the release of organic pollutants in wastewater, rendering sewage treatment critical. Currently, sewage treatment methods include filtration, sedimentation, flocculation, aeration, biological and chemical degradation, disinfection, photocatalysis, and other methods [1–4]. Among these, photocatalysis has attracted attention as a promising technology that utilizes light radiation and photocatalysts to decompose pollutants [5,6]. While P25, a popular and commercial-grade titanium dioxide (TiO2), is non-toxic, hydrophilic, and highly thermally stable, its large bandgap restricts its use of solar light to only the ultraviolet band. Moreover, the small specific surface area and the tendency of P25 particles to aggregate limit the utilization of active sites, leading to the rapid recombination of photo-induced carriers and the reduction of photocatalytic performance. Similarly, other conventional photocatalysts also face limitations, such as high toxicity, easy aggregation, wide bandgap, slow transfer rate, and easy recombination of photo-induced carriers [7–10]. Accordingly, there is an urgent need to explore novel photocatalysts that can improve the efficiency of degrading organic pollutants.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), composed of metal clusters and organic linkers, have garnered significant attention as photocatalysts due to their tunable structure and optical properties, high surface area, well-defined pore structure, and facile functionalization [11]. Various MOFs constructed using metal nodes, such as titanium (Ti), copper (Cu) [12], and iron (Fe) [13], have been explored and demonstrated for photocatalytic applications. Ti, in particular, has become an attractive candidate element due to its low toxicity and redox capability [14–16]. However, pure NH2−MIL-125 has a combination rate of the photo-induced carriers that is too high to conduct the photocatalysis efficiently. Accordingly, composite materials of TiO2 and NH2−MIL-125 could address the issue and take advantage of each other.

Zhao et al. and Huang et al. prepared P25/NH2−MIL-125 by solvothermal method for the visible light photocatalytic oxidation of cyclohexane and degradation of formaldehyde, respectively [17,18]. Combining NH2−MIL-125 with P25 via the heterostructure between these two materials, they found that the electron-hole recombination could be reduced, and the photocatalytic performance was better than that of using NH2−MIL-125 or P25 individually. Ahmadpour et al. also used the solvothermal method to disperse Ca onto P25/NH2−MIL-125. The presence of an appropriate amount of Ca could not only further reduce the probability of electron-hole recombination but also effectively reduce the energy gap and improve the photocatalytic degradation of organic compounds in water [19]. Similarly, Wang et al. used the solvothermal method to disperse the prepared CdS nanorods into the NH2−MIL-125 precursor titanium butoxide (C16H36O4Ti) and 2-aminoterephthalic acid (2-amino benzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid, NH2-BDC) for self-assembly. This approach not only effectively reduced the electron-hole recombination but also enhanced the effect of photocatalytic degradation of RhB [20]. Emam et al. prepared Ag3VO4/NH2−MIL-125 using the solvothermal method, which significantly improved the speed of electron transfer to the surface, followed by participating in the photocatalytic degradation of MB and RhB [21]. Ao et al. used a solvothermal method to disperse Cu(NO3)2·3H2O into C16H36O4Ti and NH2-BDC for self-assembly. The formation of doping energy levels made it easier to transfer the excited electrons to participate in chemical reactions [22]. In this study, we synthesized NH2−MIL-125 and its composite materials via a facile reflux method, departing from the solvothermal synthesis method mentioned earlier. In contrast to the solvothermal method, the reflux method can be conducted under atmospheric pressure, thereby reducing production costs for future applications.

In addition, the incorporation of carbon materials into MOFs, such as CQDs@NH2−MIL-125 [23], CQDs@MIL-125 [24], graphene quantum dots modified NH2−MIL-125 [25], graphitic carbon nitride modified NH2−MIL-125 [26], has gained significant attention. These carbon materials enhance photocatalytic efficiency by facilitating effective electron-hole separation and enabling up conversion luminescence. This enhances the conversion of near-infrared light into visible light, thereby improving light utilization efficiency. Accordingly, we also incorporate CQDs to further augment the photocatalytic performance of the CQDs/P25/NH2−MIL-125 composite materials. The design of CQDs/P25/NH2−MIL-125 composite materials is outlined as follows. First, NH2−MIL-125, with a high specific surface area, can disperse P25 and CQDs while preventing particle aggregation. Second, the heterostructure of NH2−MIL-125 and P25 accelerate the separation of photo-induced electrons and holes. Third, the up-conversion property of CQDs is employed to convert longer wavelength light energy into shorter wavelength, expanding the range of light radiation that can be utilized. Accordingly, even near-infrared light can be converted into visible light, significantly improving solar light utilization. On the other hand, the good conductivity of CQD can promote the efficient separation of photo-induced electrons and holes, thereby improving the overall efficiency of the photocatalytic reaction. As a result, the CQDs/P25/NH2−MIL-125 composite materials capitalize on the strengths of each component, synergistically offering superior capabilities for the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants.

The chemicals used in this study were in the analytical reagent grade. 97 % titanium(IV) isopropoxide(TTIP) and 98.0 % methyl blue (MB) were provided by Sigma- Aldrich. 99.5 % dimethylformamide (DMF) and 99.0 % NH2-BDC were obtained from Alfa Aesar. 98.0 % rhodamine B (RhB) was offered from Acros Organics. 99.8 % methanol (CH3OH) was purchased from Fisher Chemical. Commercial P25 (anatase: 80 % and rutile: 20 %) was supplied by UniRegion Bio-Tech. CQDs, synthesized by the thermal pyrolysis of aniline via the hydrothermal method, were provided by Prof. Wei-Yu Chen (National Pingtung University of Science and Technology, Taiwan).

Most previous studies have primarily focused on the synthesis of NH2−MIL-125 or its composite materials using the solvothermal method [3,17,19,20,22,27-29]. In contrast, this study aims to compare the solvothermal and reflux methods for the initial synthesis of NH2−MIL-125. Interestingly, our results demonstrated that NH2−MIL-125 prepared using the reflux method exhibits higher crystallinity, reduced agglomeration, a lower band gap, and improved chemical stability, as evidenced in the supplementary data I. Consequently, all subsequent sample preparations mentioned below would adopt the reflux method. This method involved the use of a reflux condenser positioned above the reaction vessel to ensure that any evaporated gas could be condensed back into the reaction mixture for synthesis. Moreover, the reflux method was conducted under ambient pressure with continuous stirring, suggesting lower equipment requirements and higher yield than the solvothermal method.

Synthesis of NH2−MIL-125: NH2-BDC, serving as the precursor for NH2−MIL-125, was dissolved in a mixture of DMF and CH3OH solvents. Next, titanium isopropoxide (TTIP), with its titanium ions serving as the metallic nodes of NH2−MIL-125, was gradually introduced into the solution. In the reflux method, the reaction mixture was at 110 °C for 72 h. Next, the resulting mixture was subjected to centrifugation, washed multiple times with DMF and CH3OH, and dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 24 h. Finally, the resulting powder was named NH2−MIL-125(R). To enhance powder stability in water [30], a post-treatment at 120 °C was conducted, and the samples were denoted as NM125(RP) to represent NH2−MIL-125(RP).

Synthesis of CQDs/P25/NH2−MIL-125: Similarly, the precursors for NH2−MIL-125 were mixed, and TTIP was gradually added drop by drop while stirring. After 1 h, P25 and varying amounts of CQDs (0 wt%, 0.5 wt%, 1.0 wt%, and 1.5 wt%) were introduced into the mixture with continuous stirring. Subsequently, a reflux reaction was conducted at 110 °C for 72 h. The reacted mixture was then subjected to centrifugation and washed multiple times using DMF and CH3OH. The resulting composite materials were dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 24 h. The obtained composite materials underwent further post-treatment and were named PNM125(RP), CPNM125(RP)−0.5, CPNM125(RP)−1.0, and CPNM125(RP)−1.5, representing different loading amounts of CQDs at 0 wt%, 0.5 wt%, 1.0 wt%, and 1.5 wt%, respectively.

X-ray diffractometry (XRD) was conducted using a BRUKER D8 Advance to analyze the crystal structure of the samples. The morphology was observed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM), specifically the JSM-6701F model. Meanwhile, the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) integrated with SEM using the ZEISS AURIGA instrument was also conducted for elemental analysis. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) instrument used to obtain higher-resolution images was JEOL JEM-2100F CS STEM. The specific surface area of the prepared samples was determined using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method with an ASAP 2020 PLUS instrument. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis was carried out using a NICOLET 380 instrument. The chemical analysis was conducted using a PHI5000 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) using an aluminum target (Al Kα) as an X-ray source. The concentrations of organic pollutants were measured using UV–Visible spectroscopy, employing the HITACHI U-2910 UV–VIS and UV-1800. Photoluminescence (PL) excitation and emission spectra were obtained using a Hitachi F-7000. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out using a Biologic SP-150 analyzer. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted using an SDT 2960 Simultaneous DTA-TGA instrument. Lastly, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) measurements were performed using the Bruker EMXplus-10/12/P/L SYSTEM.

The photocatalytic degradation experiments were performed using a 300 W xenon lamp with an AM1.5 G optical filter, which simulated solar light. To maintain a light intensity of 100 mW/cm2, a light intensity meter (Newport Model 1919-R) was utilized. Initially, 0.05 g of the prepared samples were added to a 20 ppm MB solution. The concentration of dyes in the solution was determined using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-2910) at characteristic absorption wavelengths of 663 nm for MB and 557 nm for RhB. The mixture underwent a dark adsorption process for 50 min, during which the concentration was sampled every 10 min. Subsequently, the solar simulator was activated to initiate the photocatalytic reaction. The concentration of the MB solution was measured at 30 min, 60 min, 90 min, 120 min, and 150 min of irradiation.

Fig. 1 (a) showed the XRD pattern of NM125(RP), demonstrating distinct peaks that indicate the successful synthesis of NH2−MIL-125 using the reflux method. Besides, the XRD pattern of PNM125 (RP) displayed characteristic peaks of both NM125(RP) and P25, suggesting that the composite materials retained the crystalline structures of NH2−MIL-125 and P25. However, with the increasing dispersion of CQDs onto the composite materials, the intensity of the characteristic peaks of CPNM125(RP) gradually decreased, as depicted in Fig. 1(b). This observation suggested that the CQDs were uniformly distributed within the pores or covered the surface of NH2−MIL-125. An alternative possibility is an occurrence of a “ship-in-a-bottle” effect [7], where a portion of NH2-BDC reacted with CQDs, forming covalent bonds during the synthesis process, thereby reducing the intensity of the peaks associated with NH2−MIL-125. Nonetheless, these findings indicated the successful fabrication of ternary composite materials.

The SEM images of P25 and the various prepared samples were presented in Fig. 2 (a) and (b)-(f). First, the SEM image in Fig. 2(b) illustrated the uniform and disk-like shape of NM125(RP) particles, with diameters ranging from 100 nm to 150 nm. Moving on to PNM125(RP), Fig. 2(c) revealed the even distribution of P25 particles on the surface of NH2−MIL-125, indicating that more active sites of P25 were exposed to contact pollutants. Additionally, the close connection between NH2−MIL-125 and P25 suggested an improvement in the charge transfer capability [18]. Subsequently, the dispersion of 0.5 wt%, 1.0 wt%, and 1.5 wt% CQDs onto NM125(RP) was observed in Fig. 2 (d-f). Compared to PNM125(RP), the particle diameters of CPNM125(RP)−0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 were significantly reduced. Also, with the increased amount of CQDs, the particles tended to aggregate more due to the formation of covalent bonds between the organic ligands and the surface of CQDs during self-assembly [5], consistent with the XRD data. Meanwhile, the elemental composition of the sample was determined through EDS analysis. The results showed that carbon (C) constituted 32.6 atomic%, nitrogen (N) accounted for 0.2 atomic%, oxygen (O) made up 49.7 atomic%, and titanium (Ti) represented 17.5 atomic% of the composition. Carbon was found to be the predominant element in the sample. The presence of carbon and nitrogen could be attributed to NH2−MIL-125 in CPNM125(RP)−1.0. Additionally, the presence of titanium could be ascribed to both the Ti element of P25 and the Ti metal nodes within NH2−MIL-125. Similarly, the presence of oxygen (O) could be attributed to the O element of both P25 and NH2−MIL-125.

The TEM image of the CPNMIL125(RP)−1.0 samples at a magnification of 40,000x was presented in Fig. 3 (a). The image confirmed the presence of both NH2−MIL125 and P25 in the samples. In Fig. 3(b), magnified at 120,000x, the distinct structure of P25 was visible, exhibiting particle sizes ranging from 23 nm to 27 nm. In Fig. 3(c), magnified at 500,000x, the coexistence of CQDs alongside P25 was further evident, with the CQDs displaying a particle size of approximately 4 nm. Moreover, upon analyzing the lattice spacing of P25, it could be determined that the observed crystal face corresponds to 〈1,1,6〉 of P25 with a crystal space of 0.372 nm. These findings provided valuable insights into the morphological characteristics and composition of the samples.

The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of the prepared samples were presented in Fig. 4 (a). By conducting BET analysis, the specific surface area and pore size information were evaluated and summarized in Table 1 and Fig. 4(b). The results demonstrated that NM125(RP) exhibited the highest BET specific surface area of 743.5 m2/g. Even with the addition of P25, PNM125(RP) still maintained a significant surface area of 665.7 m2/g, indicating the uniform loading of P25 particles onto NH2−MIL-125. A larger specific surface area generally signified an increased probability of contact between the catalysts and pollutants, while the pore size could selectively adsorb specific pollutants. However, upon loading CQDs onto PNM125(RP), a significant decrease in the specific surface area was observed, suggesting that the tiny CQDs particles became embedded within the structures of PNM125(RP) and obstructed the pores of NH2−MIL-125. Additionally, the involvement of CQDs might hinder the self-assembly of NH2−MIL-125 due to mutual covalent bonding, leading to a lower specific surface area. With the addition of 0.5wt% CQD, the specific surface area of BET became significantly smaller. With the further addition of 1.0wt%, the specific surface area reaches the minimum. Consequently, this effectively mitigates the issue of excessive adsorption capacity. Conversely, the excess addition of 1.5 wt% CQDs to the sample might result in the CQDs aggregation and uneven dispersion within the MOF pores due to an unsatisfactory proportion of CQDs during the self-assembly process. As a result, CPNM125(RP)−1.5 still exhibited high porosity and specific surface area. Notably, CPNM125(RP)−1.0 exhibited the smallest pore volume of 0.06 cm3g−1, nearly one-tenth of the pore volume of 0.56 cm3g−1 for NM125(RP). This effectively addressed the issue of excessive adsorption capacity. Nevertheless, NM125(RP), PNM125(RP), and CPNM125(RP)−0.5, 1.0, 1.5 exhibited a hysteresis curve at higher relative pressure in Fig. 4(a), indicating the presence of a mesoporous structure. Such mesoporous structures demonstrated higher selectivity for the adsorption of mixed organic pollutants, such as MB and RhB, during the photocatalytic reaction test [31].

As shown in Fig. 5 , the FTIR spectra of the prepared samples revealed the presence of specific molecular structures and functional groups, confirming the successful synthesis of CQD/P25/NH2−MIL-125 composite materials. In the spectra, the interval between 600 and 800 cm−1 represented the O-Ti-O bonds in the TiO2 clusters. The bands observed at 1253 cm−1 and 1337 cm−1 were attributed to the CN bonds in the organic ligands. Additionally, the peaks at 1382 cm−1 and 1582 cm−1 corresponded to the symmetric vibrations of the carboxylate ligands, while the peaks at 1435 cm−1 and 1533 cm−1 presented the asymmetric vibrations of the carboxylate ligands. Of particular significance, the peak observed at 1621 cm−1 indicated the presence of the C = O group in the aromatic amines, which was a characteristic functional group of NH2−MIL-125 [32]. The appearance of these peaks confirmed the successful synthesis of NH2−MIL-125 and its composite materials using the facile reflux method. Upon the uniform dispersion of P25 on the surface of NH2−MIL-125, the characteristic peak of the C = O group at 1621 cm−1 shifted slightly to 1645 cm−1, suggesting a successful synthesis and a strong interaction between P25 and NH2−MIL-125 [32]. Subsequently, with the addition of 1wt% CQDs, a new characteristic peak emerged at 1778 cm−1, corresponding to the C = O absorption of the carboxylic acid functional group presented in the CQDs [33]. This peak further confirmed the successful synthesis of the ternary composite materials, CQD/P25/NH2−MIL-125.

XPS analysis was performed to investigate the chemical composition and oxidation state of the samples. The general XPS spectra of NMIL125(RP), PNM125(RP), and CPNM125(RP)−1.0 indicated the presence of C, N, O, and Ti, as shown in Fig. 6 (a). The C 1 s spectra, depicted in Fig. 6(b), displayed four distinct peaks. These included a peak at 284.5 eV corresponding to C = C double bonds, a peak at 285.1 eV representing CC single bonds, a peak at 286.2 eV indicating CO bonds, and a peak at 288.6 eV signifying CN bonds. The CO and CC peaks were attributed to the organic linker (terephthalic acid), while the C = C double bond peak was associated with the benzene ring. These peaks' similar intensities and positions for all three samples suggested that the C 1 s signals mainly originated from the MOF components. In the N 1 s spectra (Fig. 6(c)), two peaks were observed at 399.4 eV and 401.8 eV, corresponding to NH2 and -NH+- groups, respectively. Interestingly, the intensities of these two peaks decreased after the introduction of CQDs, implying that CQDs might have replaced the NH2 site in constructing the composite materials. In Fig. 6(d), the O 1 s spectra exhibited two distinct peaks: one at 530.8 eV representing Ti-O bonds and the other at 532.2 eV originating from the OH bonds in terephthalic acid. Notably, the Ti-O signal showed a slight shift after adding P25 and CQDs, which has also been observed in other studies involving P25/NH2−MIL125 [34] and CQDs/NH2−MIL125 [23,35]. Furthermore, the Ti 2p spectra (Fig. 6(e)) displayed two distinct peaks, identifying as Ti 2p2/3 at 459.3 eV and Ti 2p1/2 at 464.5 eV. Similarly, the composite material exhibited a slight shift in the Ti 2p2/3 position, consistent with previous literature findings [34]. These shifts toward lower binding energies might indicate that photoexcited electrons were more likely to separate from Ti-O or Ti, facilitating electron-hole separation.

Fig. 7 (a) illustrated the UV–visible spectra of different samples, providing insights into their light absorption properties. Pure NM125(RP) exhibited visible light absorption with an absorption edge at approximately 550 nm. The addition of P25 resulted in a slight blue shift in the absorption edge of PNM125(RP), but the change was significant. However, upon uniformly dispersing CQDs on P25 and NH2−MIL-125 to form ternary composite materials, an increase in absorption in both the visible and near-infrared regions was observed. This indicated that CQDs effectively enhanced light absorption across a wider range of solar light. It was worth noting that adding 1 wt% CQDs demonstrated the highest absorption ability. It is speculated that an appropriate amount of CQDs ensured their uniform distribution within the samples, resulting in improved light absorption in the visible range and increased efficiency for photocatalytic degradation under visible light.

Furthermore, the Tauc plot analysis in Fig. 7(b) revealed the bandgap values of NM125(RP) and PNM125(RP) as 2.45 eV and 2.50 eV, respectively. The small difference indicated that the addition of P25 had minimal impact on the light absorption properties of the prepared samples, which was consistent with previous literature [32]. In contrast, the introduction of CQDs led to a decreasing trend in the bandgap values. The bandgaps of CPNM125(RP)−0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 were determined as 2.30 eV, 2.00 eV, and 2.20 eV, respectively. It was observed that CPNM125(RP)−1.0 exhibited the smallest bandgap, inferring the uniform dispersion of 1 wt% CQDs into the pores of NH2−MIL-125. Additionally, CPNM125(RP)−1.0 exhibited the darkest color, indicating its superior light absorption in the visible range. This enhanced light absorption enabled more efficient excitation and improved separation of photo-induced electron-hole pairs, thus contributing to enhanced photocatalytic performance.

During the photocatalytic degradation process of organic pollutants using a photocatalyst, effective adsorption of pollutants onto the active sites of the photocatalyst was crucial before subsequent photocatalysis could take place. The adsorption mechanism of pollutants onto NH2−MIL-125 could be categorized into four types: (1) electrostatic interaction, (2) π-π stacking, (3) hydrogen bonding, and (4) pore filling [36]. To investigate the favorable adsorption of cationic MB pollutants onto the active sites, the zeta potential was analyzed to determine the surface charge of the photocatalysts under different acidic and alkaline conditions. Fig. 8 (a) illustrated the zeta potential values of NM125(RP) under various pH conditions. The isoelectric point was determined to be 5.75, indicating that the catalyst surface carried a positive charge below the isoelectric point and a negative charge above it. In the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants, a lower recombination rate of electrons and holes within the photocatalyst led to improved photocatalytic degradation efficiency.

By analyzing the Rct resistance using EIS Nyquist plots, the charge transfer impedance within different materials could be examined. A larger semicircle diameter obtained from the materials indicated a greater charge transfer impedance, hindering the electron transfer process. In Fig. 8(b) and Table 2 , it could be observed that both NM125(RP) and P25 exhibited large semicircle diameters, with Rct values of 896.84 Ω and 1782.0 Ω, respectively. This indicated high resistance within these two materials, unfavorable for photo-induced electron transfer. However, when a controlled amount of P25 was uniformly dispersed on the surface of NH2−MIL-125, the semicircle diameter of PNM125(RP) decreased, and the charge transfer impedance Rct significantly decreased to 137.05 Ω. This suggested that the close heterostructures between P25 and NH2−MIL-125 effectively promoted electron transfer, consistent with a previous study [37]. Furthermore, it is noticeable that the semicircle diameter of CPNM125(RP)−1.0 slightly increased compared to PNM125(RP), and the charge transfer impedance (Rct) increased from 137.05 Ω to 219.8 Ω. This could be attributed to the slight aggregation phenomena caused by the addition of CQDs. However, the photo-induced electrons could still be effectively and rapidly transferred. Consequently, the close combination of the ternary composite materials CQDs/P25/NH2−MIL-125 facilitated the efficient transfer of excited electrons to the surface, reducing the likelihood of electron-hole recombination and thereby enhancing the photocatalytic degradation performance.

Furthermore, the probability of electron-hole recombination within the materials could be analyzed using PL spectra. A higher PL intensity indicated a higher likelihood of electron-hole recombination. In Fig. 9 (a), the PL intensity of PNM125(RP) exhibited the lowest value, indicating that the formation of the heterostructures with the addition of P25 effectively promoted electron transfer, resulting in reduced PL intensity. This observation was consistent with the impedance reduction observed in the PNM125(RP) heterostructures. However, upon adding different amounts of CQDs, the PL intensity of CPNM125(RP)−0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 significantly increased. This suggests that CQDs facilitate enhanced energy absorption by the composite materials, leading to a relatively higher probability of electron-hole recombination and increased PL intensity. Despite some loss of photo-induced carriers due to recombination, a substantial number of electrons still transfer to the surface for further reactions. Schematic diagrams illustrating this phenomenon were depicted in Fig. 9(b). Among the different amounts of CQDs added, CPNM125(RP)−1.0 exhibited the lowest PL intensity, indicating the highest electron transfer rate and the lowest probability of electron-hole recombination. Additionally, PL spectroscopy was employed to confirm the up-conversion effect of the CQDs. As shown in Fig. 10 , the CQDs used in this study exhibited light irradiation-induced excitation in the visible and near-infrared light region, with an excitation wavelength range of 500 nm to 850 nm. Through the up-conversion effect, a material could absorb two photons of lower energy and emit a photon of higher power with a shorter wavelength. Thus, the prepared CQDs demonstrated a noticeable shift in emitted light towards shorter wavelengths, validating the presence of the up-conversion effect. For instance, in the blue curve, the excitation wavelength was set at 600 nm, and the emission wavelength was observed at approximately 400 nm.

To understand the primary active radicals involved in photocatalysis, EPR was utilized to validate their presence in the samples. It provided valuable evidence regarding the photocatalytic capabilities of the synthesized materials. As shown in Fig. 11 (a), the EPR analysis of CPNM125(RP)−1.0 confirmed the presence of superoxide radicals (O2·−) under light irradiation, while the signals observed in the dark condition exhibited relatively low intensity. By comparing the EPR signals of different samples, it could be deduced that the synthesized samples were capable of producing O2·− under light irradiation, thereby confirming their photocatalytic abilities, as shown in Fig. 11(b). In contrast, the EPR analysis to detect hydroxyl radicals (OH·) did not exhibit any signals for all the samples, even under light irradiation. Therefore, it could be concluded that O2·− was the primary radical produced by the samples under light irradiation, which played a significant role in conducting the photocatalytic MB degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective adsorption of mixed MB/RhB solutions
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selective adsorption behavior of the prepared NM125(RP), a uniform solution containing a mixture of MB and RhB was prepared. Subsequently, 0.05 g of NM125(RP) was added to the solution under continuous stirring, and the concentration of the solution was measured every minute for a total of ten measurements. As shown in Fig. 12 , NM125(RP) demonstrated a higher adsorption capability towards MB than RhB, which could be attributed to the pore size effect [36]. The size of the pollutant molecules played a crucial role in determining the adsorption capacity. Notably, the molecular size of RhB was approximately four times larger than that of MB [31], resulting in NM125(RP) exhibiting a higher selectivity towards MB in the mixed solution. This observation aligned with previous studies that highlighted the influence of pore size on adsorption selectivity [31,38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance and kinetics analysis of CPNM125(RP)−0.5, 1.0, 1.5
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 13 (a), it was found that CPNM125(RP)−1.0 and CPNM125(RP)−1.5 exhibited a decrease in dark adsorption capacity. Conversely, CPNM125(RP)−0.5 maintained a strong adsorption capability, with a significant reduction in MB concentration within 30 min. This suggested that an appropriate amount of CQDs was crucial, effectively filling the pores in CPNM125(RP) and facilitating photocatalytic reactions instead of dark adsorption. With CQDs loading exceeding 1.0 wt%, the dark adsorption capacity gradually diminished, while the photocatalytic reaction capability became prominent. Among these composite materials, CPNM125(RP)−1.0 exhibited the lowest dark adsorption capability but the highest photocatalytic performance. Additionally, the kinetic analysis of photocatalytic MB degradation was shown in Fig. 13(b). Based on first-order kinetics analysis, as shown in Eq. (1).
(Eq. 1)
− l n
( C /
C 0
)
=
k 1
t

The rate constants for different CPNM125(RP)−0.5, 1.0, and 1.5, obtained using the first-order model, were presented in Table 3 . The results showed that CPNM125(RP)−1.0 exhibited the highest rate constant, k1=0.0141 min−1, indicating its superior photocatalytic performance compared to CPNM125(RP)−0.5 and 1.5. In addition to the first-order kinetic analysis, the second-order kinetic analysis for CPNM125(RP)−1.0 was also conducted. However, the obtained R2 value was 0.9286, which was lower than the R2 value of 0.9982 obtained from the first-order kinetic analysis. Therefore, it could be inferred that CPNM125(RP)−1.0 synthesized in this study effectively represented the mechanism of photocatalytic MB degradation through the first-order kinetic model. Accordingly, this finding suggested that the addition of 1 wt% CQDs could enhance light absorption and facilitate photo-induced electron transfer, thereby resulting in more effective photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants in the environment.

It was essential to note that if dark adsorption dominated the pollutant removal process, the surface and pores of the photocatalysts would be inactive after adsorbing excessive pollutants, leading to a decline in photocatalytic performance. The adsorption capacity of the photocatalysts had a specific limit and reached equilibrium after a certain period, beyond which it could not adsorb more pollutants. Second, when the dark adsorption occurred too fast, the photocatalyst surface became heavily covered by a thick layer of organic pollutants, seriously compromising its light absorption ability and hindering efficient photocatalytic degradation. Therefore, for effective and sustainable degradation of organic pollutants, photocatalysts should possess sufficient adsorption capacity and excellent photocatalytic performance. In this study, CPNM125(RP)−1.0 demonstrated suitable characteristics as a ternary material serving as a photocatalyst. Moreover, various MOF composite materials for photocatalysis were reported in the literature for comparison, as shown in Table 4 . Variations were observed in terms of MOF photocatalysts used, pollutant degradation, and light sources employed, resulting in diverse photocatalytic performances. However, most literature reports employed the solvothermal method for MOFs synthesis, which required a high-pressure autoclave. In contrast, in this study, a reflux method was utilized for MOFs preparation, which could be performed under ambient conditions without requiring specialized equipment. Moreover, the synthesis of MOFs, CQDs, and P25 was carried out in a single reactor, significantly reducing the complexity of the synthetic steps. This simplified process holds promise for future large-scale production and utilization of catalytic materials.

Therefore, the photocatalytic mechanism of the composite material (CPNM125(RP)−1.0) was proposed as shown in Fig. 14 . Firstly, NH2−MIL-125 in the composite material exhibited a high specific surface area, enabling efficient adsorption of pollutants from water, thereby contributing to the subsequent photocatalytic process. Additionally, the presence of CQDs in the catalysts enhanced the light absorption, as confirmed by UV–vis and up-conversion PL measurements. The CQDs could absorb more visible light and convert longer wavelength light into shorter wavelength light through up-conversion, thereby providing more light for the photocatalytic reaction. Furthermore, EIS and PL emission spectroscopy results indicated that the composite material reduced surface resistance, and an appropriate amount of CQDs incorporation reduced the probability of electron-hole recombination, which were both beneficial for photocatalytic degradation. Consequently, upon simulated sunlight irradiation, CPNM125(RP)−1.0 could generate a significant number of effective electron-hole pairs. The holes reacted with OH− to produce hydroxyl radicals (·OH), while the electrons reacted with oxygen to generate superoxide radicals (O2·−). Based on the EPR, it was evident that the main radical generated in this study was the superoxide radical, which possessed strong oxidative capabilities and effectively degraded MB pollutants from water. To sum up, the synergistic effect of NH2−MIL-125′s adsorption capacity, CQDs' light absorption properties, and the generation of reactive radicals enabled efficient and effective photocatalytic MB degradation. This proposed the mechanism for the excellent photocatalytic performance observed in our composite materials. Further investigations and optimizations could be explored to enhance the photocatalytic efficiency and expand its applications in water purification and pollutant degradation. For instance, the durability test, the effect of concentration of dyes, catalyst loading, and pH on the degradation of MB using CPNM125(RP)−1.0 would be discussed in supplementary data II.

This study employed a facile reflux method to simplify the synthesis of NH2−MIL-125. Besides, it was demonstrated that the post-treatment of the samples at 120 °C improved the stability in water. Subsequently, various composite materials of P25/NH2−MIL-125 and CQDs/P25/NH2−MIL-125 were developed to remove MB pollutants from water efficiently. Based on the SEM and TEM images, EIS, XPS, and PL emission results, it was speculated that P25 particles were well-distributed on the surface of NH2−MIL-125, forming heterostructures that reduced the resistance of charge carriers transfer and facilitated the separation of photo-induced electrons and holes. In the case of CQDs/P25/NH2−MIL-125, the UV–vis spectra exhibited a significant red shift due to the involvement of CQDs, resulting in a reduction of the samples’ bandgaps. Unlike P25, CQDs would not only disperse on the surface of NH2−MIL-125 but also embed into the pore structure of NH2−MIL-125, leading to a weakening of XRD patterns and porous structures of NH2−MIL-125. Notably, CQDs exhibited the up-conversion effect, effectively converting long-wavelength (λ> 500 nm) light into short-wavelength (λ< 500 nm) light, thereby allowing P25 and NH2−MIL-125 to generate more photo-induced electrons. Moreover, an adequate additional amount of CQDs was essential, as it influenced the dispersion of CQDs on the composite materials and the light absorption of the samples. The addition of CQDs effectively prevented the rapid occupation of the surface by pollutants and the fast deactivation of the photocatalysts. In summary, the incorporation of 1 wt% CQDs into P25/NH2−MIL-125 offered several advantages, including enhanced adsorption capacity and excellent photocatalytic capability. It was further observed that 1 wt% CQDs/P25/NH2−MIL-125 exhibited the highest efficiency in photocatalytic MB degradation, with the first-order kinetics rate constant (k1) of 0.0141 min−1.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC), Taiwan, for the financial support under grant number MOST 110–2221-E-006–217-MY3. Special thanks to Prof. Li-Heng Kao (Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering, National Kaohsiung University of Science and Technology) and Prof. Chung-Wei Kung (Department of Chemical Engineering, National Cheng Kung University) for supporting the resource of instruments. The authors also thank the facility support from NSTC 112–2740-M-006–001 and MOST111–2731-M-110–001.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2023.105286.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of titanium based hetero MOF photocatalyst for reduction of Cr (VI) from wastewater

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Dhivya, D. Magadevan, Y. Palguna, T. Mishra, N. Aman, Synthesis of titanium based hetero MOF photocatalyst for reduction of Cr (VI) from wastewater, J Environ Chem Eng, 7 (2019) 6.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Supporting carbon quantum dots on NH2-MIL-125 for enhanced photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants under a broad spectrum irradiation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q.J. Wang, G.L. Wang, X.F. Liang, X.L. Dong, X.F. Zhang, Supporting carbon quantum dots on NH2-MIL-125 for enhanced photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants under a broad spectrum irradiation, Appl Surf Sci, 467 (2019) 320–327.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of 3D N-CQD/MOF-5 photocatalyst to improve the photocatalytic performance of MOF-5 by changing the electron transfer path

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.T. Qin, T.T. Qiang, L. Chen, S.T. Wang, Construction of 3D N-CQD/MOF-5 photocatalyst to improve the photocatalytic performance of MOF-5 by changing the electron transfer path, Microporous Mesoporous Mat., 315 (2021) 10.
